fullText: china just unveiled a humanoid robot face that looks almost too human Google DeepMind is now teaming up with agile robots to bring Gemini into real factory machines McDonald's in Shanghai and San Jose airport are already putting humanoids in front of customers MIT just built a wristband that can control robotic hands with your own movements Titan01 is copying human actions in milliseconds And in Chicago a delivery robot just smashed through a glass bus shelter It's been another wild week in AI and robotics so let's get into it All right let's start with what might actually be the most unexpected direction here and that's the face A video that started circulating recently shows a humanoid robot developed in China with extremely lifelike facial expressions This wasn't just a stiff robot with basic movements It was blinking naturally scanning the room reacting to what it saw and showing subtle emotional changes in real time The clip came from Yu Hong Hu the founder of Showing Technology and it picked up a lot of attention online because of how realistic it looked For years most of the focus in robotics has been on movement strength and industrial capability Can the robot lift heavy objects can it walk properly can it operate in a factory that's been the main benchmark Yet now a growing number of researchers and companies are starting to say that none of that is actually the biggest challenge The real challenge is making robots socially acceptable Companies like a Head Form have already been working on this They released a humanoid platform called Elf V1 back in October 2025 which also focused heavily on facial expressions and reaction-based interaction And the idea is pretty simple when you break it down Robots that can do physical work already exist In many cases a simple robotic arm can do those jobs faster and cheaper So if humanoid robots are going to succeed especially in public-f facing roles they need something more They need to feel human That's where these new systems come in These robots are being built with synthetic skin microactu under the surface and AI models that control facial expressions in real time They can blink track faces react to conversations and adjust their expressions based on context When paired with multimodal AI systems like the Omni AI platform mentioned in demonstrations they can see hear and respond in a way that feels natural during interaction And what's starting to become clear is that the face might end up being the most important part of the robot Not the legs not the arms but the ability to communicate emotion Because if these machines are going to work in places like malls museums or customer service environments the way they interact with people matters just as much as what they can physically do At the same time there's still a debate inside the industry Some experts see these humanoid robots as more of a performance showcase right now something that looks impressive yet isn't economically efficient Others are convinced that making robots more humanlike is the only way they'll ever be widely accepted in everyday life And while that's happening another completely different direction is moving forward in parallel and that's industrial robotics powered by large AI models A company called Agile Robots based in Germany just announced a collaboration with Google DeepMind The goal here is to combine DeepMind's Gemini Robotics Foundation models with Agile Robots existing hardware systems And this isn't about making robots more humanlooking This is about making them smarter and more capable in realworld production environments But AI isn't only about machines interacting with the physical world It's also becoming a powerful tool for creating entirely new ones HPCAI is sponsoring today's video and they just launched a new feature called model APIs which makes it really easy to start building with powerful open models without dealing with complicated setup Right now you get access to models like Kimmy K2.5 and Miniax M2.5 which are great for coding long context tasks and building AI agents The key thing here is that it's OpenAI compatible So if you've used OpenAI APIs before you can plug this into your existing projects almost instantly That also makes it a really good fit for tools like Open Claw You can connect the API test different models build assistance automate workflows or even prototype simple AI apps pretty fast Getting started is simple You sign up pick a model and start testing No payment required You get $2 in free credits by default and if you use my link you get $4 total to test everything out Go check it out Link is in the description All right now let's get back to the video Agile Robots has already deployed over 20,000 robotic systems globally so they're not starting from scratch They have a full lineup that includes robotic hands force sensitive arms like the F FR3 the Diana 7 arm and the Thor series Their new humanoid platform called Agile 1 is designed to work alongside humans in industrial settings safely and efficiently Now when you combine that hardware with something like Gemini Robotics you get a very different kind of system Instead of a robot that follows fixed instructions you get one that can adapt learn from real world data and improve over time They're calling this an AI flywheel Basically the robots get deployed into real environments They collect operational data That data is used to train and refine the AI models And then those improved models make the robots better which allows for broader deployment And that cycle just keeps going The collaboration is structured in multiple phases where the systems are continuously tested improved and redeployed And the initial focus is on high value industrial applications especially in areas that require reliability and scalability This also connects to a broader strategy from DeepMind They've been expanding partnerships across the robotic space Earlier this year they announced work with Boston Dynamics to enhance the Atlas humanoid robot using Gemini models They're also working with Aptronic on the Apollo humanoid platform And intrinsic innovation has joined Google as a separate unit focused on robotics So while one part of the industry is trying to make robots more human on the outside another part is making them far more capable on the inside And then you've got cases where both of these directions start showing up in realworld environments For example at San Jose Manetta International Airport there's now an AI powered humanoid robot named Jose operating inside terminal B near Gate 24 This robot was developed by a startup called Inbot and it's designed specifically for passenger interaction It can greet travelers answer questions provide directions and it supports more than 50 languages That's a big deal in an international airport where people from all over the world are trying to navigate a complex environment The robot uses a combination of perception reasoning and natural interaction to understand what people need and respond accordingly And this is part of a 4-month pilot program to test how these systems perform in busy public spaces City officials are looking at this as a way to improve customer experience accessibility and overall efficiency especially with events like the FIFA World Cup expected to bring in large numbers of international visitors At the same time Intbot is framing this as part of a bigger shift toward what they call social intelligence in physical AI The idea is that robots need to understand human intent context and behavior not just follow commands You're seeing something similar in China as well where humanoid robots are already being used in customerf facing roles There was a recent demonstration inside a McDonald's in Shanghai where robots developed by Kenon Robotics were greeting customers taking orders delivering food and even interacting with diners in real time They were wearing the standard red and yellow uniforms blending into the environment while still being clearly robotic What stands out here is that these robots aren't just doing back-end tasks They're right in front of customers handling roles that were traditionally considered difficult to automate because they require interaction and communication And there's a broader context behind this According to reports companies like Amazon already have over 1 million robots in their warehouses and about 75% of their global deliveries are assisted by robotics in some way So automation is already deeply integrated into logistics and operations Now it's moving into direct human interaction At the same time this shift isn't happening without push back In Chicago there was a recent incident where a delivery robot operated by Serve Robotics crashed through a glass bus shelter The video that went viral shows the aftermath with the robot covered in shattered glass and debris scattered across the sidewalk No injuries were reported and the company said their team responded quickly and is reviewing what happened Yet incidents like this are adding to growing concerns about the safety of autonomous machines in dense urban environments Residents have already been complaining about delivery robots crowding sidewalks and creating obstacles There are also concerns about onboard cameras and data collection in public spaces In fact more than 80% of residents in a local survey opposed the idea of robots operating in their neighborhood and over 3,600 people have signed a petition calling for a citywide ban This isn't an isolated case either In Miami a delivery robot ended up on train tracks and was destroyed by an oncoming train In Los Angeles robots have been overturned or disabled by activists And in one case a robot entered an active police scene which caused confusion before it was identified as a false alarm So while the technology is advancing quickly the real world integration is still messy At the same time there are also major advancements happening in how robots move and learn physical tasks A Chinese startup called Westlake Robotics introduced a humanoid robot named Titan01 powered by a system they call the general action expert or GAE model This model acts like a central coordination system similar to a cerebellum in the human brain During a demonstration a human operator wore a motion capture suit and performed actions like waving turning and kicking a ball The robot mirrored those movements almost instantly within milliseconds matching everything from arm swings to step length with high precision The system can also control multiple robots at the same time with each one performing identical tasks in sync And it's designed to adapt to different operators meaning it can handle variations in movement without losing accuracy One of the key features here is what they call the shadow function where the robot essentially becomes a realtime physical extension of the human operator This opens up possibilities for remote work safer operations in dangerous environments and much faster training compared to traditional programming methods Instead of manually coding every movement the robot learns by observing and replicating human actions And that ties into another breakthrough coming out of MIT where researchers developed a wearable ultrasound wristband that can track hand movements with extremely high precision This device uses small ultrasound sensors to image the internal structures of the wrist including muscles tendons and ligaments It then uses AI to translate those internal changes into digital signals representing 22 degrees of freedom in the hand So every finger movement every subtle adjustment can be captured and translated in real time The system was trained using thousands of data points combining camera footage with ultrasound imaging to map internal muscle activity to specific hand positions It was tested on eight different volunteers and was able to accurately reproduce complex movements including American Sign Language gestures and precise grips like holding a pencil or scissors What's interesting is how this connects back to robotics The wristband can be used to control robotic hands directly essentially acting like a puppet system where your hand movements are mirrored by a robot in real time It also has potential applications in virtual and augmented reality where natural hand tracking is a major challenge And then you've got the broader visibility of these systems increasing as well At a recent AI summit at the White House a humanoid robot appeared alongside Melania Trump walking with her and greeting attendees in multiple languages It was described as the first American-made humanoid robot to be presented in that setting The event brought together representatives from 45 countries and focused on topics like AI and education digital literacy online safety and the impact of technology on child development There were also discussions about risks including deep fake technology and the need for safeguards especially for younger generations Concerns about screen exposure and social media use were raised as well The presence of the robot at the summit was mostly symbolic Yet it highlighted how quickly these systems are moving from labs into public and policy spaces And when you look at all of this together it becomes clear that robotics is no longer just one thing All of these directions are moving forward at once and the pace is only picking up If you enjoyed this breakdown drop a comment with your thoughts and subscribe for more Thanks for watching and I'll catch you in the next one
